coredata.dc:description: An intermediate-scale reactor with 10L capacity and two transducers operating at 700 and 950 kHz frequencies was developed to study the scalability of the sonolytic destruction of Per and Polyfluoroalkyl substance (PFAS). The impact of frequency, height of liquid or power density, and transducer position on reactor performance was evaluated with the potassium iodide (KI) oxidation and calorimetric power. The dual frequency mode of operation has a synergistic effect based on the triiodide concentration, and calorimetric power. The triiodide concentration, and calorimetric power were higher in this mode compared to the combination of both frequencies operating individually. The sonochemical efficiency for an intermediate-scale reactor (10L) was similar that obtained from a bench-scale reactor (2L), showing the scalability of the sonolytic technology. The placement of the transducer at the bottom or side wall of the reactor had no significant impact on the sonochemical reactivity. The superposition of the ultrasonic field from the dual transducer mode (side and bottom) did not produce a synergistic effect compared to the single transducer mode (bottom or side). This can be attributed to a disturbance due to the interaction of ultrasonic fields of two frequencies from each transducer. With the encouraging results scaling up is in progress for site implementation.

head.ce:title: Contributions of reactor geometry and ultrasound frequency on the efficiency of sonochemical reactor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An intermediate-scale reactor with 10L capacity and two transducers operating at 700 and 950 kHz frequencies was developed to study the scalability of the sonolytic destruction of Per and Polyfluoroalkyl substance (PFAS). The impact of frequency, height of liquid or power density, and transducer position on reactor performance was evaluated with the potassium iodide (KI) oxidation and calorimetric power. The dual frequency mode of operation has a synergistic effect based on the triiodide concentration, and calorimetric power. The triiodide concentration, and calorimetric power were higher in this mode compared to the combination of both frequencies operating individually. The sonochemical efficiency for an intermediate-scale reactor (10L) was similar that obtained from a bench-scale reactor (2L), showing the scalability of the sonolytic technology. The placement of the transducer at the bottom or side wall of the reactor had no significant impact on the sonochemical reactivity. The superposition of the ultrasonic field from the dual transducer mode (side and bottom) did not produce a synergistic effect compared to the single transducer mode (bottom or side). This can be attributed to a disturbance due to the interaction of ultrasonic fields of two frequencies from each transducer. With the encouraging results scaling up is in progress for site implementation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acoustic cavitation is a phenomenon associated with the propagation of intense sound waves in liquids generating bubbles [1]. This phenomenon involves three discrete stages: nucleation, bubble growth, and, under proper conditions, implosive collapse [2,3]. The implosive collapse of cavitation bubbles cause sonophysical effects such as microjet, microstreaming, shear force, acoustic emissions (shock waves), and sonochemical reactions that include pyrolysis and radical reactions [4–6]. Sonochemistry is a multidisciplinary field of chemistry and physics dealing with the concentrated energy in the short-lived (several nano second duration) cavitation bubbles. Although the use of ultrasound as the primary means of stimulating chemical reactions and for the water and wastewater treatment has been known for many years, this safe form of irradiation and emerging technology has become increasingly popular during the last two decades [4]. The sonochemical degradation of Per and Polyfluoroalkyl substance (PFAS) and other emerging pollutants in an aqueous solution has been proven to be a successful bench-scale technology [7–12]. Most reported studies in the literature have used small-volume or laboratory-scale ultrasonic reactors (volume < 1 L) [7–9,13–17]. The challenges in scaling up the sonochemical process to meet field application needs for treatment of PFAS are the volumetric flow rates, reaction energy rates, efficiencies, and the overall cost [18,19].

PFAS are a large family of human made fluorinated organic compounds used for over 90 years in many applications and its recalcitrant nature have led to their frequent detection in the environment [20–23]. PFAS have been designated as contaminants of emerging concern since the early 2000 s [24,25].The
C - F
covalent bond is the shortest and strongest in nature and is responsible for the thermal and chemical stability of PFAS [26]. In conjunction with tension-lowering capabilities, this property makes PFAS ideal for a wide range of industrial and commercial applications such as non-stick coating, textiles, paper products, and some firefighting foams [22]. However, these very same properties rendered PFAS to be bio-accumulative, environmentally persistent, and resistant to the conventional water and wastewater treatment methods, leading these compounds to be nicknamed “forever chemicals” [27–29]. Global studies have identified significant concentrations of PFAS at both urban and rural sites on all six continents, with many sites located at far distances from potential sources, such as in the Antarctic [30–35].

In 2023, the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) proposed to establish legally enforceable levels, called maximum contaminant levels (MCLs), for six PFAS in drinking water [36]. Proposing MCL of 4 parts per trillion (PPT) for perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctanoate sulfonic acid (PFOS) as individual contaminants, and 1 ppt MCL for perfluorohexane sulfonic acid (PFHxS), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), perfluorobutane sulfonic acid (PFBS), and hexafluoropropylene oxide-dimer acid (HFPO-DA) (commonly referred to as GenX Chemicals) as a PFAS mixture. Lenka et al., 2021, compiled the latest information on the worldwide distribution of PFAS, including the United States and evaluated their fate in wastewater treatment plants [37–39]. PFAS were widely detected in both the influents (up to 1,000 ng/L) and effluents of WWTPs ranging from 15 to > 1,500 ng/L [38]. Hu et al., 2016, analysis of PFAS concentration in public water supplies and had identirfied PFAS, particularly PFOS and PFOA at or above the EPA 2023 published MCLs in 194 of 4864 public water supplies, serving 16.5 million residents in 33 different states [40]. Conventional water and wastewater treatment technologies (e.g., air sparging, enhanced aerobic bioremediation, etc.) have shown limited success in mineralizing PFAS, as PFAS are uniquely recalcitrant and persistent [8]. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), which use hydroxyl radicals such as Fenton’s reagent (
H 2
O 2
and
Fe
2 +
) and ozone (
O 3
), have shown limited effectiveness in the mineralization of PFAS due to the strength of the C-F bonds and the high electronegativity of fluorine [7,8,41].

Current practices for treating PFAS contaminated water are limited to sorption/sequestration separation technologies with granular activated carbon (GAC) and ion exchange (IX) resins [42–46]. These approaches can process extremely large volumes of liquid with low PFAS concentrations, typically in the parts per trillion (ppt) or in ppb ranges, typical with water and wastewater treatment plants. The IX treatment approach is gaining popularity over GAC due to its higher adsorption capacity, shorter contact time, on-site regeneration capability to a nearly virgin quality, smaller equipment footprint, wider range of PFAS adsorbed, and the ability to concentrate PFAS contaminated waste into the parts per million range (ppm) [42]. The concentrated solution (called brine solution, still bottoms, or high concentration PFAS waste) resulting from IX regeneration contains high concentrations of PFAS (typically in the parts per million range), salt, and residual organic compounds [42,47,48]. There are also emerging separation technologies such as reverse osmosis (RO) and foam fractionation for PFAS removal, however, they also generate concentrated waste streams which require further processing [49,50].

Many advanced oxidations process including high-frequency ultrasound technology at lab-scale have shown varying degrees of effectiveness in degrading PFAS in water [51]. While promising, these technologies need extreme operating conditions, centralized laboratory equipment, and have high chemical and energy requirements, impeding their practicality and widespread use [52]. However, optimizing PFAS remediation by combining concentration and eventual destruction in a treatment train can offer energy-efficient and cost-effective options for decision-makers. This coupled approach can eliminate risks associated with high concentration PFAS waste generated from IX, RO, or form fractionation by removing both the environmental and human health hazards in addition to potential off-site environmental liability.High-frequency ultrasound technology coupled with a separation method as a treatment train approach can effectively mineralize PFAS. The sonolytic process, driven by propagating acoustic waves in liquids at frequencies ranging from 400 kHz to 1000 kHz, is shown to be both a practical and relatively fast method for the mineralization of perfluorooctanesulfonic acid (PFOS), perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), and other PFAS [3,7,8,12,14,53–56].

The geometric parameters of large-scale reactors vary in number and location of transducers, frequency of operation, shape of the reactor, power dissipation per unit volume, and cavitational yield. The geometry of the reactor and transducer parameters play an important role in determining the effectiveness of the sonochemical reactor as it dictates the distribution of cavitational events. Son et al., 2009 and Son et al., 2011 demonstrated that a small target volume (or low liquid height) is not effective for enhancing the sonochemical reaction in an ultrasonic application [57–59]. Asakura et al., 2008, investigated the sonochemical efficiency of a sonochemical reactor as a function of frequency and liquid height. This study varied the liquid height to evaluate different power densities in the reactor [60]. Son et al., 2009 and Lim et al., 2014 found that a change in the liquid height could result in a significant change in the sound energy intensity and sound energy distribution [58,59] . It has been reported that multifrequency transducers can significantly improve sonochemical efficiencies when compared to single-frequency transducers [61–63].

Large-scale sonochemical reactor have been used in the literature, with volumes ranging from 7L to 100L [18,60,64–66]. Gonze et al., 1998, developed a 28L capacity sonochemical reactor in which they had three 500 kHz transducers attached to the bottom [66]. Destaillants et al., 2001, used four 612 kHz transducers and developed a 7L capacity sonochemical reactor [18]. Similarly, Matsuoka et al., 2007 and Paquin et al., 2013, developed 133 kHz [65] and 170 kHz [64] reactors, respectively. The pilot study by Gole et al., 2018a,b investigated a large-scale (91L capacity) multi-transducer sonochemical reactor with dual frequencies to treat PFOS and PFAS in the AFFF, respectively [10,67]. The reactor contained 12 transducers (three 500 kHz and nine with 1 MHz operating frequency) connected simultaneously connected to six generators. Most large-scale reactors in previous studies used single frequency transducers. The impact of power density and liquid height was not evaluated in any of those studies. The reactors were not optimized and thus unit treatment costs were high. Hence full-scale development of high frequency sonolytic reactors for PFAS and field applications has yet been demonstrated. Optimization of the reactor geometry and transducer location will minimize the operation costs and enhance the acceptability of sonochemical reactors for commercial applications.

In this study, an intermediate scale 10L reactor operating at dual frequencies (700 kHz and 950 kHz) was designed, fabricated and evaluated for optimization. The impacts of the frequency, liquid height, power density, transducer location, and superposition of the ultrasonic field was evaluated using KI oxidation and calorimetry power and then some of the findings were confirmed using actual PSAF degradation tests. The sonochemical efficiency for several different configurations and operations was also evaluated and compared with that of a laboratory scale sonochemical reactor for the design of an efficient pilot-scale sonoreactor.

Potassium iodide (>98%) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Milli-Q water (>18MΩ·cm) was used throughout the experiment. PFOA (>98.0%) and undecafluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA) was purchased from TCI America. Potassium perfluoroctanesulfonate (K-PFOS) (≥98.0%), heptafluorobutyric acid (PFBA) (≥98.0%), nonafluorobutane-1-sulfonic acid (PFBS) (≥98.0%), and tridecafluorohexane-1-sulfonic acid (PFHxS) (≥98.0%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Ammonium acetate, HPLC-grade methanol, total ionic strength adjustment buffer (TISAB II), and fluoride with TISAB II standards were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. The USEPA 533 PFAS standards were purchased from Wellington Laboratories.

A novel intermediate scale 10L reactor was designed and fabricated in consultation with PCT Systems, Inc, San Jose, California. The walls of the reactor were designed to use replaceable transducer plates. Two custom multi-frequency transducer plates were purchased from PCT Systems, Inc, San Jose, California, each comprising six piezo elements of 6 inches by 1 inch. Each transducer plate consisted of an array comprising three groups of two piezo elements, one piezo element of 950 kHz resonant frequency and the other of 700 kHz resonant frequency. The first transducer plate was attached to the bottom of reactor and second plate was attached to one of the side walls of the reactor, as shown in figures S1 and S2. The reactor contained 316 stainless steel cooling coils with electropolished finish of 1⁄4″ diameter tubing, as shown in figures S1. The cooling coil was connected to the endocal RT 100 chiller. Two PCT 6000 series radiofrequency (RF) generator were also procured from PCT Systems to supply electrical power to the piezo element in the reactor. Each RF generator supplies power to each resonant frequency crystal in both transducers. The transducers were operated in the multiplex mode. Each RF generator supplied electrical power to each piezoelectric elements in both transducer for one second, followed by a shift in power to the second piezoelectric elements in each array for another second, and so forth.

Calorimetry method has been widely used to quantify acoustic energy delivered into the reaction medium and to represent ultrasonic power in sonochemistry [54,60,68–70]. In calorimetry method, thermal energy delivered to the medium is measured as a function of ultrasonic irradiation time for determining ultrasonic power.

In the above equation,

C p

Δ T

is the rate of change of temperature over time. Thus, by measuring thermal energy delivered to the medium as a function of ultrasonic irradiation time, the efficiency of the sonoreactor can be evaluated. The ratio of ultrasonic power Pu to the electrical driving power Pe,
P u
P e
is defined as the calorimetric efficiency.

The calorimetry test was performed using milli-Q water and the samples were aerated for 30 min before testing. For all tests, the sonication time was set to 10 min. During this test, no cooling water was circulated in the cooling coil in the tank to prevent heat dissipation and to accurately determine the change in temperature. The temperature change was measured at the center and at the mid-depth of the reactor. The current and volage reading were directly taken from electric meter attached to the RF generator to compute the power consumption.

The KI dosimetry method to quantify the cavitational activity is based on the fact that iodide ions in an aqueous solution can be transformed into iodine molecules during sonication [69–71]. When the ultrasound waves were irradiated in the aqueous solution of KI, the iodide ion

The sonochemical efficiency (SE) is defined as the number of chemical species formed or depleted per unit ultrasonic energy delivered to the solution in the sonochemical reactor. In practice, the amount of SE is calculated from the following equation using the amount of substance
m ( m o l )

Here, Q (J) is ultrasound energy,
P u
is the Ultrasonic Power and t is time.

The KI dosimetry test was performed using a 10 g/L KI solution. The formation of triiodide ion was analyzed using ultraviolet (UV)-vis spectrophotometry at 355 nm wavelength. The solution was aerated for 30 min before testing [71–73], and the sonicated for 10 min. Duplicate samples (of approximately 10 ml) were collected from two locations in the reactor using a disposable dropper or pipette, one sample from the bottom of the tank and the other sample from the top of the tank. Samples were collected at the beginning and end of the 10-minute test. The solution was allowed to cool to room temperature before analyzing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sonolytic treatment of synthetic high-concentration PFAS waste
ce:section.ce:para: Reagent grade PFAS was mixed with milli-Q water to prepare the synthetic high-concentration PFAS waste (in ppm range, typical with still-bottom or reject from RO and NF). During this test, cooling water was circulated in the cooling coil in the tank for dissipating heat generated during sonication. The solution was sonicated in fifteen-minute cycles. Total treatment time in these sets of experiments was set to four hours, unless otherwise mentioned. Duplicate five mL samples were collected in a 15 ml polypropylene vials every sixty minutes over the full treatment time for each test. These samples were collected from the sampling port in the reactor for PFAS and fluoride ion analysis. During the treatment of the high concentration waste stream, the pH of the solution was not controlled. The initial pH of the solution, based on different initial concentrations, ranged from 5 to 5.5. After treatment, the pH of the solution was reduced to a range of 3.8 to 4.5, resulting in an acidic solution.

The PFAS mineralization in the waste stream was determined using triple quadrupole LC/MS (Agilent 6470 Triple Quadrupole LC/MS System), and Fluoride-ISE ion tests. The reactor was cleaned three times with tap water, two times with DI water, and one time with methanol and DI water solution (70:30 v/v) before starting a new experiment to prevent any contamination that may be present from the previous experiment. An equipment blank was also collected after the methanol and DI water solution wash to ensure no PFAS contamination of the reactor.

All water samples were diluted with methanol and with a dilution ratio such that the final concentration was below 100 parts per billion (ppb). Diluted samples were filtered using a 0.25 μm polyether sulfone (PES) syringe filter before analysis by LC/MS. Targeted PFAS analysis of liquid samples was completed following a modified USEPA 533 method using liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry [74]. The instrumental detection limit was 0.0001 mg/L, with method detection limits varying based on matrix and sample processing requirements. The sonochemical efficiency was calculated using the G value method by Campbell et al., 2015 and Wood et al., 2020 [9,54]. The G-value was calculated using Eq. (4), based on mass:
(4)
G
Value
=
V ∗
C
PFAS , 0

C
PFAS , t
P ∗ t
( g K W
h
- 1
)
where V is the treatment volume (L), CPFAS,t is the PFAS concentration at the end of the reaction (g L-1), CPFAS,t is the PFAS concentration at the start of the reaction (g L-1), P is the power drawn into the reactor (kW), and t is the reaction time (h).

To test the concentration of inorganic fluoride in solution, a fluoride-ion selective electrode (F-ISE) from Thermo-scientific was used. Liquid samples were diluted with TISAB II in a 1:1 ratio. If the expected fluoride concentration in the sample was above the upper limit of the calibration curve, the sample was diluted with a DI water and TISAB II solution.

In the custom built reactor, the KI dosimetry, calorimetry and PFAS experiments were performed for three modes of transducer operation shown below to evaluate the impact of frequency on the distribution and characteristics of acoustic cavitation.
•
700 kHz piezo element plate at the bottom
•
950 kHz piezo element plate at the bottom
•
Both 700 and 950 kHz piezo elements plate together at the bottom

For these tests, the reaction volume was kept at 4.25L and the power rating of transducer was adjusted to 100%. Only three piezo elements were used for the first two modes of operation, while for the last mode of operation, all six piezo elements were used. For the first two modes of operation, one resonant frequency of either 700 kHz or 950 kHz on the array was enabled. As mentioned before, the transducer was operated in multiplex mode of operation where power was cycled through each of the three piezo elements of the corresponding frequency in the array at 1 s interval of each piezo crystal. The multiplex mode of transducer operations was used as in this mode of operation would prolong the life of the transducer because the operation cycle of each transducer is lowered. For the last mode of operation, both frequencies were enabled, with one element of each frequency being powered at the same time. Thus, for the first two modes of operation, electrical power was supplied to one piezo element at a time, whereas for the last mode of operation, electrical power was supplied to two piezo elements at a time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of frequency mode on the KI dosimetry and calorimetry test
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1 A shows the electrical power drawn for each mode of operation and shows that the 700 kHz piezo element consumed more energy than the 950 kHz piezo element. This corresponded with an increase in the concentration of triiodide ions
I
3
-
(Fig. 1B) and the calorimetry power as well (Fig. 1A). However, the sonochemical efficiency for 950 kHz piezo element was higher than that for 700 kHz by 66%, as shown in Fig. 1B. For the dual mode operation (700 kHz and 950 kHz), the concentration of triiodide (14.50 µM), was 14% higher than the combined concentration for 700 kHz and 950 kHz modes of operation (12.68 * 10-06 µM). Similarly, the calorimetric power for the dual mode of operation (333 W) was 8% higher than combined calorimetry power for 700 kHz and 950 kHz mode of operation (309 W). These results indicates that the dual frequency mode of operation has some synergistic effect as the triiodide concentration and calorimetric power are higher in this mode when compared to the combination of both frequencies operating individually. However, the sonochemical efficiency for the dual mode of operation was lower than that for the 950 kHz mode of operation but higher than the 700 kHz mode of operation as shown in Fig. 1B.

Yasuda et al., 2007 and Suzuki et al., 2004 [75,76] observed the synergistic effect for ultrasonic reactor with dual frequency, however, in both studies different frequency transducer was along different side of reactor resulting in the superposition of ultrasonic field generating possible standing waves from two ultrasonic transducer. The rectangular reactor used by Yasuda et al., 2007 had two 50 mm transducers with one attached to the center of bottom wall and other was attached to the center of left-hand side of reactor wall. The ultrasonic field was varied from 176 to 635 kHz. Whereas the rectangular reactor used by Suzuki et al, 2004 had one 50 mm transducer attached to the center of bottom wall and other horn transducer placed at the center of liquid height. The ultrasonic field for plate transducer varied from 24 to 1170 kHz and frequency for horn transducer was 20 kHz. Unlike these two studies, the work presented in this research both frequencies transducers were attached to the bottom wall of reactor hence the impact of the wave superposition was eliminated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of frequency mode on the sonochemical degradation of high concentration synthetic PFAS waste
ce:section.ce:para: The sonochemical degradation of six PFAS synthetic high concentration waste using an intermediate scale 10L reactor for three modes of transducer operation was evaluated to understand the impact of frequency on the degradation of PFAS and compare the reactor performance for sonochemical degradation with the calorimetry efficiency and sonochemical efficiency. Please note that the transducers at both the bottom of reactor was used during this test to treat 4.25L of waste. The initial concentrations of total PFAS in the waste stream was 18 ppm with individual concentrations for each compound being 1/6 of the total or 3 ppm. The PFAS concentration and fluoride were monitored to evaluate the mineralization efficiency and formation of end-products during sonochemical treatment. Fig. 2 shows the decrease in the individual PFAS concentrations over time for three mode of transducer operations. Fig. 3 shows the decrease in the total PFAS concentration and release of fluoride ion concentration over time for three modes of operation. In general, long-chain PFAS decreased in concentration faster than short-chain PFAS. The PFBA showed the slowest removal rate of all chemicals tested. This can be attributed to the increasing hydrophobicity of short-chain PFAS, which results in a reduction in the affinity of the molecule for the water-bubble interface. This in turn causes a reduction in pyrolytic degradation [14].

Although the Perfluoroalkyl carboxylates acids (PFCAs) are less hydrophobic and surface active than perfluoroalkyl sulfonate acids (PFSAs) with the same number of carbon atoms [77], the results showed that carboxylates degraded faster than sulfonates. Similar trends were reported by Campbell and Hoffman, 2015 and Fernandez et al., 2016. This can be attributed to the lower thermal activation energies for carboxylates [9,14]. It has also been suggested in a recent experimental study by Psillakis et al., 2009 that there are conformational restrictions to interfacial enrichment of PFSAs when compared to PFCAs at the water–air interface of micro/nanobubbles formed during sonication when the perfluoroalkyl chain length was above 4 [78]. These results together with those presented in Figs. 2 and 3 indicate that the hydrophilic functional group had a weaker PFAS degradation rate, with carboxylates degrading slightly faster than sulfonates with the same perfluorocarbon chain length.

Unlike other PFAS destructive treatments, during ultrasound treatment, no significant increase in the concentration of short-chain PFAS, namely PFBA, PFBS, PFHxA, PFHxS were observed. Also, other short-chain PFAS not present in the synthetic waste namely perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA) and perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA) were not detected in the treated waste stream [79,80]. The degradation of PFAS leads to the generation of fluoride ions, which increased over time during the PFAS sonolytic treatment. Fig. 3B shows the fluoride ion generation over time for three different mode of transducer operation. The concentration of highest fluoride increased was observed for 700 kHz + 950 kHz. The release of fluoride followed zero-order reaction kinetics and rate of formation of fluoride averaged 0.36*10-3 mMmin−1, 0.24 *10-3 mMmin−1, and 0.23*10-3 mMmin−1 for three mode of transducer operation: 700 kHz + 950 kHz, 700 kHz, and 950 kHz respectively. The percentage degradation, based on reduction of PFAS mass in waste stream were, 14.3%, 9.0%, and 5.2% for three mode of transducer operation: 700 kHz + 950 kHz, 700 kHz, and 950 kHz respectively. The percentage defluorination achieved based on fluoride release rate were 15.5% 9.8%, 6.7% for three mode of transducer operation: 700 kHz + 950 kHz, 700 kHz, and 950 kHz respectively. The percentage defluorination achieved was slightly higher than percentage degradation achieved suggesting complete degradation of PFAS into the end products
SO
4
2 -
,
CO
2
, C O , a n d
F
-
[7–9,14,15,81].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of sonochemical degradation of high concentration synthetic PFAS waste with KI oxidation and calorimeter power
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 shows a comparison of the energy efficiencies for sonochemical degradation of six PFAS for three mode of transducer operation, As shown in Table 1 for dual frequencies mode, a higher concentration of triiodide was observed in the dual mode of operation than the combined concentration for 700 kHz and 950 kHz mode of operation. Similarly, for the total fluoride release and fluoride release rate for the combined frequency mode (700 kHz + 950 kHz) were higher than that for individual frequency modes of operation. However, as in the case of the KI dosimetry test, a reduction in sonochemical efficiency was observed for the combined frequency (700 kHz + 950 kHz) when compared to 950 kHz for the PFAS test, a reduction in the G value was observed for the combined frequency mode was comparatively lower than that for 950 kHz transducer mode of operation.

Above test results show that the KI dosimetry and calorimetry test can be used as indirect methods for optimization of reactor performance for PFAS destruction. Evaluating the reactor performance for PFAS destruction tests can be long and tedious and hence using KI dosimetry and calorimetry can be used as indirect test method for evaluating the reactor performance and scaling up technology for field application the PFAS destruction and hence KI dosimetry and calorimetry methods were used for remaining tests.

In this test, one transducer plate was attached to the bottom of the tank for one trial and then moved to one of the side walls for the next trial. The purpose of this test was to determine the potential effect of the reflection of ultrasonic waves back into the reactor. When the transducer is placed on the side wall, the ultrasound waves will hit the opposite wall made of stainless steel, a material which has a higher acoustic impedance than water. When the transducer is placed on the bottom of the reactor, the ultrasonic waves will hit the surface of the water and move to the air, which has a lower acoustic impedance than water. This movement from lower to higher or higher to lower acoustic impedances may have different effects on each system and impact the sonochemical efficiency. For these tests, the reaction volume was kept at 8.5L and the transducer operated in the dual-mode with a power rating adjusted to 100%.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the placement of the dual-frequency transducer along the bottom and side wall of the reactor had no significant impact on the sonochemical activity as evident from the fact that the calorimetry power and sonochemical efficiency remained similar for both different transducer positions. Similar results have been reported by Yasuda et al., 2007 [75]. This can be explained by the rapid decay of ultrasonic waves as they pass through the liquid medium. Once the waves reach the opposite side of the reactor, they have already attenuated to such a degree that reflection has no significant impact for a reactor of this size.

In this test, one transducer plate attached at the bottom of the tank, operating in the dual mode was used and power rating was adjusted to 100%. The power density was varied by adjusting the treatment volume (2.125L, 4.25L, 6.375L and 8.5L) of the solution while maintaining the same power levels (100% power rating). As shown in Fig. 5 A, with the reduction in the reaction volume, the electrical power level remained constant, a slight reduction in the calorimetry power was observed and calorimetry for this test varied from 56% for 8.5L reaction volume whereas it was reduced to 42% for 2.125L. This corresponded with a slight reduction in the sonochemical efficiency, as shown in Fig. 5B. A further reduction with liquid volume can result in the reduction in calorimetric efficiency. With an increase in the power density, the formation of triiodide ion increased (Fig. 5B). For 8.5L volume the measured concentration for triiodide was 9 µM whereas for the 2.125L the measured concentration was 22 µM. However, total moles for triiodide formed were 78.4 µmol, 70.3 µmol, 61.6 µmol and 47.5 µmol for 8.5 L, 6.375 L, 4.25 L and 2.125 L respectively. This implies increasing the power density increased the amount of sonochemical activity but not proportionally. Son et al., 2009 and Son et al., 2011 have shown that for a small target volume (or low liquid height) change in reactor volume would not change the sonochemical reaction [57–59]. Also, Son et al., 2009 and Lim et al., 2014 found that a change in the liquid height could result in a significant change in the sound energy intensity and sound energy distribution [58,59].

Table 2 shows the comparison for the 2 L bench scale reactor used in the Kewalramani et al 2022 [82] and compare the performance for the 10 L reactor used in this study for different power densities. The results show the sonochemical reactor performance of the bench-scale reactor (2 L) used in the Kewalramani et al., 2022 [45] was comparable to that of the intermediate-scale (10L) reactor, showing the potential for scaling-up of technology for large-scale operation.

In this test, one transducer plate attached at the bottom of the tank, operating in the dual mode was used and power rating on the transducer plate and reaction volume was varied to maintain the constant power density to evaluate the impact of the sample height and volume of liquid on reactor performance. To maintain constant power density, power rating (electrical power supplied) was varied. The applied electrical current for 8.5L, 6.375L, 4.25L and 2.125L were 678 W, 708 W, 516 W and 246 W respectively. The measured electrical power densities for 8.5L, 6.375L, 4.25L and 2.125L were 80, 111, 121 and 116 W/L respectively. Literature search showed that no previously reported research maintained the power density by varying the power supplied for reaction volume/height of liquid experiments. As shown in Fig. 6 A, with a reduction in the reaction volume, the electrical power reduced to maintain a similar power density level and that resulted in a reduction in the calorimetry power as well.

For a constant power density, it was observed that the calorimetric efficiency reduced significantly with a reduction in the reaction volume. When the total input power was kept constant (Fig. 6 A), a 14% reduction in calorimetry efficiency was observed for a volume reduction from 8.5L to 2.125L. However, when the power density was kept constant (Fig. 6A), the efficiency reduced from 55% to 21%. Thus, having a very small volume in the reactor has a detrimental impact on the reactor performance, regardless of the power density.

For 8.5L volume the measured concentration for triiodide was 9.2 µM whereas for the 2.125L the measured concentration was 9.6 µM. However, total moles for triiodide formed were 78.4 µmol, 70.2 µmol, 53.6 µmol and 20.4 µmol for 8.5 L, 6.375 L, 4.25 L and 2.125 L respectively. For both the constant power level and constant power density tests, a reduction in the reaction volume resulted in an increase in the concentration of triiodide until the sample volume was 4.25L. However, total moles of triiodide formed decrease with reduction in reaction volume in both test. As expected, for constant power density, a similar sonochemical efficiency was obtained until the sample volume was 4.25L, as shown in Fig. 6B. The measured ultrasonic sonochemical efficiencies (using Pu) for 8.5L, 6.35L and 4.25L were 4.35*10-10, 3.97*10-10, and 4.35*10-10 M/J, respectively. For the reaction volume of 2.125L, the measured ultrasonic sonochemical efficiency (using Pu) was 6.55*10-10 M/J.

In this test, one transducer plate was attached at the bottom of the tank and second plate was attached to the one of the side walls, operating both transducer plate in the dual-mode at 100% power ratings. As shown in Fig. 7 , with the superposition of the ultrasonic field from the transducer attached to the side and bottom of the reactor, there was an increase in the concentration of triiodide and the calorimetry power. For this dual transducer mode (side and bottom) the calorimetry power and triiodide concentration were 650 W and 13.45 µM respectively, whereas for the bottom transducer mode, these values were 375 W and 9.23 µM. The measured calorimetry power and triiodide concentration for dual mode of operation 73% and 69% respectively higher than single transducer mode (bottom), thus no synergistic effect was observed. As shown in Fig. 7B, the sonochemical efficiency for the dual transducer mode was lower than the sonochemical efficiency for single transducer, implying the superposition of the ultrasonic field can reduce the sonochemical activity in the solution. Results reported here are corroborated by Yasuda et al., 2007 [75], who reported if the two frequencies in the system (176 and 635 kHz) are significantly different, the ultrasonic field produced from the transducer attached to side can disturb the ultrasonic field propagating from the bottom of the tank. In the current set of experiments, the dual frequencies can be assumed to sufficiently different that the ultrasonic fields interfered with each other.

An intermediate scale (10L) multi-frequency ultrasonic reactor was designed, fabricated and used. The effect of frequency, liquid height (power density), and transducer position on KI oxidation and calorimeter power were examined. The following conclusions were obtained:
•
The energy conversion efficiency from electricity to ultrasound was about 50%.
•
For the dual frequency mode of operation (680 kHz and 950 kHz), the formation of triiodide was higher than that for the combined frequency than those operating individually.
•
An increase in the power density by reducing the reaction volume resulted in an increase in the overall sonochemical activity, however, the rate of increase slowed as the power density became larger.
•
The sonochemical efficiency for an intermediate-scale sonochemical reactor was similar to that for a bench-scale reactor with a slightly higher sonochemical efficiency achieved for the intermediate-scale reactor.
•
For constant power density but different reaction volume tests, the measured sonochemical activity showed that the power density has lessor impact on the sonochemical activity, however, lower reaction volume does impact the sonochemical activity for the constant power density.
•
The placement of the transducer along the bottom and side wall of the reactor had no significant impact on the sonochemical activity.
•
The superposition of the ultrasonic field from the dual transducer mode (side and bottom) does not have a synergistic effect compared to the single transducer mode (bottom or side). In fact, the dual transducer mode resulted in a reduction of the sonochemical activity in the solution compared to the single transducer. The reduction in the sonochemical activity can be attributed to a disturbance due to the interaction of ultrasonic fields of both frequencies from each transducer.
